notes.note: Ambitus was the crime of electoral bribery; this was the second prosecution Appius beat in 50 BC, after the maiestas charge treated in Fam. 3.11.

notes.anchor: even in the secrecy of the ballot
notes.note: Roman juries voted by secret written tablets (tabellae), so a hostile juror could condemn without exposure; Cicero marvels that not even this cover for malice produced a vote against Appius.

notes.anchor: what has been done in my absence by my people
notes.note: A guarded reference to the betrothal of Cicero's daughter Tullia to P. Cornelius Dolabella, arranged by Cicero's household while he was away in Cilicia; the awkwardness is that Dolabella was at that very moment prosecuting Appius.

notes.anchor: this connection of marriage was announced to me
notes.note: The new adfinitas (relationship by marriage) created by Tullia's match with Dolabella; Cicero insists it would only have made him defend Appius more openly, not less.

notes.anchor: with my year of command now ended
notes.note: Cicero's annual proconsular imperium over Cilicia expired around 30 July 50 BC, and he was sailing home; provincial commands were normally held for a single year.

notes.anchor: the fourth day before the Nones of August
notes.note: A Roman calendar date reckoned inclusively back from the Nones (5 August), i.e. 2 August 50 BC.
Side, a harbour town on the coast of Pamphylia in southern Asia Minor, a stage on Cicero's voyage home from his province.
